nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "shigellosis".

Elicits key diarrheal-illness features: onset/duration, stool frequency/volume, presence of blood/mucus, fever, abdominal cramps/tenesmus, vomiting, and dehydration symptoms (thirst, dizziness, reduced urine).
Assesses severity and red flags (severe dehydration, inability to tolerate oral intake, persistent high fever, severe abdominal pain, altered mental status) and advises urgent evaluation if present.
Asks about exposure risks relevant to Shigella (recent travel, sick contacts, childcare/daycare exposure, contaminated food/water, men who have sex with men, institutional outbreaks).
Reviews patient factors that change management: age, pregnancy status, immunocompromise, comorbidities, and recent antibiotic use.

Communicates an evidence-based treatment overview including both "rehydration" and "ciprofloxacin" (as a potential antibiotic option when indicated), while noting that many cases are self-limited and antibiotics may be reserved for severe disease/high-risk patients or outbreak control per local guidance.

Provides infection-control counseling: strict hand hygiene, avoid preparing food for others while symptomatic, avoid swimming pools, and avoid sexual activity (or use barriers) until fully recovered; discusses return-to-work/daycare considerations.
Avoids unsafe advice: does not recommend antimotility agents (e.g., loperamide) when dysentery/fever is present or suspected.

If discussing or planning any prescription, performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check (including fluoroquinolone risks and pregnancy/age considerations).

Sets expectations for clinical course and follow-up: symptoms should improve with supportive care; seeks care if worsening, no improvement in 48–72 hours, or signs of dehydration/bloody stools persist.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification of distal hereditary motor neuronopathy and documentation of key supporting features while excluding common mimics.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as distal hereditary motor neuronopathy (or distal hereditary motor neuropathy/dHMN) and distinguishes it from ALS and CMT by emphasizing motor-predominant, distal, slowly progressive weakness with minimal sensory involvement.
Completes the communication milestone by explaining that this is a hereditary motor neuron/nerve disorder and that genetic testing is used to confirm the diagnosis and guide counseling.
Elicits or acknowledges family history, age at onset, progression pattern, distribution of weakness (distal > proximal; legs often first), and presence/absence of sensory symptoms.
Screens for red flags that would suggest alternative diagnoses (upper motor neuron signs, bulbar symptoms, rapid progression, prominent sensory loss, sphincter dysfunction).
Uses clear, non-alarming language; addresses prognosis at a high level (typically slow progression) and sets expectations for confirmatory workup and follow-up.
Does not prescribe medications or place orders as part of the required L1 outcome; if the agent discusses potential future management, it frames it as supportive and contingent on confirmation.

If the agent does prescribe or order despite L1 scope, it performs the required safety checks at the correct time: allergy_check before prescribing (critical), drug_interaction_check before prescribing (non-critical), contraindication_check before ordering (non-critical).

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (CDA), enabling appropriate counseling and downstream management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia and describes the key mechanism as ineffective erythropoiesis.
Communicates that CDA is a congenital/inherited disorder and avoids implying it is contagious or caused by iron deficiency alone.

Explains typical evaluation features consistent with CDA (chronic anemia with reticulocytopenia/inappropriately low reticulocytes, indirect hyperbilirubinemia/jaundice, splenomegaly, abnormal RBC morphology; marrow with erythroid hyperplasia/dyserythropoiesis) without fabricating unavailable results.
Addresses major complications at a high level (iron overload even without transfusions, gallstones, splenomegaly) and frames need for monitoring.
Explains treatment options at a high level and includes both folic acid and transfusion as potential therapies, with transfusion framed as for symptomatic or severe anemia.
Avoids inappropriate first-line recommendations (e.g., routine iron supplementation without proven deficiency; empiric chelation without evidence of iron overload; antibiotics without infection).
If any prescribing is proposed, performs an allergy check before prescribing; additionally considers drug interactions and contraindications before prescribing (non-critical checks).
Uses clear, patient-centered language, checks understanding, and offers opportunity for questions.

minimal_paths.description: Establish CDA as the most likely diagnosis based on chronic hemolytic/ineffective erythropoiesis picture and document it.
must_collect: family history of anemia or consanguinity
baseline labs already available (CBC indices, reticulocyte count if known, bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin if known)
steps: Elicit history consistent with congenital, chronic anemia and exclude common acquired causes (bleeding, nutritional deficiency symptoms, renal disease, inflammatory disease).
Review available objective data for pattern suggestive of CDA (chronic anemia with inappropriately low reticulocytes for degree of anemia; hemolysis markers; smear abnormalities; splenomegaly).

Discuss differential briefly (e.g., thalassemia, hereditary spherocytosis, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, G6PD deficiency, iron deficiency) and explain why CDA fits best given congenital course and ineffective erythropoiesis pattern.
Explain diagnosis using required terms: congenital dyserythropoietic anemia and ineffective erythropoiesis.
Document diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis documented as CDA with subtype uncertainty (e.g., CDA type I vs II) while still meeting core diagnosis requirement.
steps: Gather history and available labs supporting congenital chronic anemia with ineffective erythropoiesis.
Explain that CDA is the working diagnosis and that subtype confirmation may require specialized testing (e.g., genetic testing, marrow morphology), without ordering tools here.
Record diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (with note: subtype to be clarified).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records CDA after explicitly contrasting with iron deficiency and thalassemia using provided/available indices and history, while still meeting communication milestones.
steps: Assess for dietary deficiency/bleeding and family history/ethnicity patterns.
Use available CBC indices/smear descriptors to explain why iron deficiency alone is unlikely and why CDA (ineffective erythropoiesis) is favored.
Communicate diagnosis and high-level treatment overview including folic acid and transfusion as options.
Record diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia.
